item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even if it is out of universe, it is absolutely great.
Don Quixote was one of my favourite novel, and you have honored it.
+1, it is very well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an underappreciated gem which should have well above six-hundred +1s. For the moment being all I can do is to give you mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh wow. I've a new favorite in the 4000s.
You've made me feel for a man who charges full tilt towards windmills. Great job.

item.title: nice!
item.content:encoded: this is one of my favorite SCP's thus far, and the first to get me to comment. Great job!

item.title: Delightful tribute
item.content:encoded:
It's very refreshing to read a good entry about metafiction with a happy ending which also serves as a great reference to one of the most important stories in the spanish literature. This is the kind of articles that made me be fully interested in the SCP universe
PD: Fuck Marques De Sade

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am I, Don Quixote, the Lord of LA MANCHA!

IIRC, Don Quixote was his author's way of deconstructing outdated tropes in fiction at the time. Now he's out there deconstructing what it means to be fictional. I love this.

I was very pleased at the twist by ending of the first offset where Don Basinhead was revealed to be Sancho all along, having made his way to the true Woebegone Knight.

item.title: Re: Author Post.
item.content:encoded:
This is really silly and fun, and while I don't know much about Don Quixote or any of the other novels, your love for them really comes through. I also really appreciate that you added not just author commentary, but also trivia.

I love seeing author's thought processes and inspiration, and I especially love long trivia. After all, what's the point of adding in these little touches if you aren't going to tell everyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was really afraid this article isn't going to do justice to don Qichote, but as soon as I saw the name Pierre Menard I knew I was gonna like it and I wasn't disappointed. Even though I usually dislike pataphysics stuff. Bravo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is truly the last article I ever expected to make me misty, but that little bit at the end of the middle iteration, just. BWUUHHHH.

also, off topic,ish, but since Justine is mentioned, I know this is pretty awful to think. but I'm not too sad about napoleon throwing de sade in prison. I already have a soft spot for the dude for being kinder and more reasonable with the Jews than pretty much any other historical figure of his stature, giving us legal rights we had virtually nowhere else at the time. I'm glad people can't be imprisoned for years nowadays just for writing out their shitty rape fantasies, but I'm not going to shed any tears for de sade either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like him
I really like him
Tear out my fingernails, one by one
I like him

I don't have
A very good reason
Since I've been with him cuckoo-nuts
Have been in season

But there's nothing I can do
Chop me up for onion stew
Still I yell to the sky, though I can't tell you why
That I like him
…
I like him
I really like him
Pluck me naked as a scalded chicken
I like him

Don't ask me
For why or wherefore
Cause I don't have a single good because
Or therefore

You can barbecue my nose
Make a giblet of my toes!
Make me freeze,
Make me fry,
Make me sigh,
Make me cry,
Still I yell to the sky,
Though I can't tell you why,
That I
Like
Him!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came here for Murphy Law.
And I got something (almost) even better.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Belated, but: Thank you. I appreciate it.

Out of all the stuff I've written for this site, this is probably my personal favorite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the first time I read this, being only passingly familiar with the central character I wasn’t able to fully appreciate it. I thought it was worth a +1 for being a pataphysics article that actually held my attention and had actually interesting characters, but not much more.

Having since had a gander at the book (or at least Grossman’s translation owing to my lacking Spanish literacy) and having seen a cracking performance of Man of La Mancha last night, I thought it was worth rereading this, and I am awed. This is one of the finest works on this entire site, the single finest piece of metafiction, the single best SCP to use a pre-existing outside character by a large, large margin.

Don Quixote vs Anafabula when

item.title: Payoff
item.content:encoded:
This wholly justified reading Don Quixote in my high school Spanish class.

Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Toaster's referring to the ending footnote reading "They could be giants", which could easily be taken as a reference to either the film or the band called They Might Be Giants. (Especially since in the movie, the line that the title is taken from is explicitly a Quijote reference.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I LOVE Don Quijote so I was immediately intrigued. The metafictional aspect got me really curious because doing Don Quijote metafiction just fits the theme of the story so well, and I wanted to see where you went with this. Partway through I started wondering if he was just going to be another Fred, but then he started coming after the Foundation and I immediately perked up again. I initially thought the notes in Iteration 1 were by Don Quijote/Alonso and the notes in Iteration 2 were by Sancho, but then I realized they were ALL by Quijote, and now the two of them are reunited and off adventuring again… This scratched a really specific itch that I didn't even know I had, and I don't think I stopped grinning the entire time I read this. (Admittedly I had to read the comments to know that Dr. Mansard was Quijote.)

A hearty +1. Though I might be biased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Harry Potter bit killed me, lmao.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my rules regarding the use of meta in fiction is that it must serve story — not the other way around. The moment that meta becomes the story, you tread on very dangerous ground. That, and you never go full meta. Not unless you're the first one doing it.

ETA: Also, glad to hear that you liked the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I first thought that this would be another one of "meta scps" that happened to feature Don Quixote, but I was pleasantly surprised to find that there was a deeper narrative than just that.

+1

item.title: Re: Reveal on click?
item.content:encoded: That would work, but I honestly have no idea how to accomplish that (or if it can be done!). I'll look into it though!

item.title: Reveal on click?
item.content:encoded: You could have the list of footnotes become visible the first time one of the numbers is tapped/clicked on. That should let mobile users read them without cluttering the desktop view.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could definitely see people not liking it for it seeming like a -J, but I still enjoyed it nonetheless.
Great read, loved the tying in of all the different works of literature over the years.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was going to upvote with just the first iteration but I decided to read the other two iterations and man. This is easily one of my favourite SCPs to date. I'm a bit ashamed to say I got a lil emotional when the footnotes showed what "Quijote's" real "quest" was about and the ending was just perfect. You don't get happy endings in skips too often. Maybe I'm biased because I'm a Spanish speaker and a Cervantes fanboy but if I could upvote this article more than once I would.

item.title: I love this.
item.content:encoded: The story is sweet, funny, and slightly puzzling. The meta and pataphysics are done well and in all the ways I like. There isn't much else I can say about this without gushing endless approval, so I'll just give my upvote instead. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like a more literarily-learned, whimsical version of SCP-2786. Alonso's antics made me smile throughout the whole work. What a guy. Hope to see him go far in the OCT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the me reading the finishing the first iteration

Spoiler
- Intensifies

He said you sound like giants.

This gave actual chills. Fucking goosebumps, no kidding. I'd upvote this article just as it is.

Now, to offset 2

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, wow — yes, the footnotes are critical to the narrative going on here.

I didn't realize that mobile footnotes didn't work — I tested this on my mobile before going with hidden footnotes and didn't have any problems with getting them to display. That being said, it might not work properly on other mobile devices. I can certainly take out the 'hidden' footnotes. I mostly wanted them hidden for aesthetic reasons, after all.

ETA: Edited the pages to make the footnotes 'visible'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this and am leaning towards upvoting. One comment, however: from the discussion, it feels like the footnotes were important to follow one narrative. However, you've hidden the footnotes, I can't reliably hover to read them on mobile, and the page source is just the iteration thing. So that was all lost on me.

I don't think having them visible would have hurt this, and it would certainly help us mobile readers.

This is my quest, to follow that star,
No matter how hopeless, no matter how far.
To fight for the right, without question or pause,
To be willing to march into Hell --
-- for a heavenly cause!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simply amazing. The ending gave me chills.
Take the +1, Hippo, you narrative wizard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not entirely sure how to respond to that; it doesn't involve any (overt) fourth wall breaks, doesn't deal with the conceit of the Foundation as fiction, and is largely about a (real-life) tragic fictional character reconciling with his only friend. Instead of using pataphysics to deal with the idea that the Foundation's universe is fictional, I'm using it to let fictional characters interact.

I can understand someone disliking this because it's a format screw (guilty as charged), or because it's too meta (also guilty), or even because they just don't like the story (fair enough) — but you think this is just a rehash of all the other pataphysics skips? What pataphysical skip does this resemble? What previously established pataphysical trope am I just redeploying, here?

(Beg your pardon if any of this sounds hostile; I don't mean it to be — I'm just trying to understand your criticism. Part of writing this was a desire to redefine pataphysics and take it to a new place. If I accept your criticism at face value, it means I've failed at this task.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me extremely happy. I loved the original Don Quixote and this… farce (in the best possible sense)… follows perfectly in its footsteps. I laughed, I felt for Sancho, and in the end I couldn't think of a more perfect ending for the tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate you saying so; writing the bit near the end (with Alonso continuing as the footnotes pleaded for him to stop and flee) was weirdly emotional for me — but I was worried it would come off as maudlin to everyone else. I'm glad that others have had a similar response!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely fantastic work.This Scip is a narrative whopper, and the transition from Boring Old Pataphysics Tedium to this incredibly moving short story has me floored. I legitimately was about to cry reading those footnotes as he kept moving through the servers. This one is gonna leave a lasting impact on me, and I didn't even know what Don Quixote was about. Big +1

item.title: Re: Author Post.
item.content:encoded:
Added spoilers/easter eggs to the author post, to clarify some of the more obscure references and jokes.

FOR TRANSLATORS:
– hide block

Links to the three pages:

  • Page 1
  • Page 2
  • Page 3

One note I'd like to make regarding the various altered works on the first page:

Pretty much all the fiction I selected is fiction relevant to an English-speaking audience. That being said, please feel more than free to replace some of these entries with fiction relevant to the audience you're writing it for. For example, if you translate this into Chinese, feel free to replace (or add) entries where Don Quixote alters texts more relevant to a Chinese audience — etc. There are only a few things to keep in mind:

  • The joke with all these texts is that each story amounts to some variation of "Quixote shows up, strikes evil down, extols knightly virtues, and everyone lives happily ever after". The only exception is The Raven, because I couldn't imagine The Raven's narrator not getting into a fistfight with Quixote over who is more beautiful (Lenore or Dulcinea).
  • Quixote presumes giants, wizards, serpents, and other 'creatures of sin' are trafficking with the Devil, and must be struck down. This is especially true with giants. In Harry Potter, Hagrid is a good giant — but when Quixote arrives, he is immediately struck down — because in Quixote's stories, there are no such things as 'good giants'.
  • The texts should occur in the order of earliest to oldest publication dates (IE, Quixote is 'moving forward' in time). Mind you, republished texts (like a copy of A Christmas Carol republished in 1996) count as 'moving forward' in time.
  • I would suggest you keep the entries short, and limit their number to seven; this is not required, though — and I'm more than comfortable just trusting translators to do their own thing for their own audience.

Finally: I imagine I speak for everyone here on the English-speaking side of the SCP-wiki when I say that we are immensely appreciative of all the hard work you do translating these pieces. Thank you!

item.title: This. is.brilliant
item.content:encoded:
I fully agree with thefran. I personally love European literature, especially the Spanish medieval one, and I enjoyed every word.

Please take a massive +1 from another hidalgo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it's meant to be a subtle 'blink-and-you-miss-it' fourth wall break — in that he effectively replaces the book with the very article we're reading (implied by the fact that the book ends the same way the article does).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? I kinda hoped he broke the fourth wall there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think i am not exaggerating when i say that this is the best article i have ever read, or will ever read, on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still running on pataphysical burnout. This really seems like a combination of every pataphysical/metanarrativistic SCP ever written, though, just with Don Quixote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: Well.. Is there an in-universe explaination for this

No; it is intended as a non-diegetic element (in other words, it is not intended to be taken as an in-universe element).

That being said — as I have made it part of all my articles — people are free to vote in response to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I uh… actually totally forgot that was the case. I guess a couple recent Pataphysics articles that treated them as a bit more real made me forget that. It's been some time since I gave OverMeta a full readthrough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! And I'll change "unknown" to "uncertain" once I get back to a computer; the ending is meant to imply that Pierre can rewrite the whole story however he likes, but I agree that uncertain is a better word choice (and leaves more possibilities open).

Also, to add to what DrBleep said: This is more an attempt to reassert what I think the pataphysics department was intended to be, rather than a hard rewrite of the idea (although, yeah, I took out the fourth wall break — because here, it clearly would add nothing).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A pataphysics/narrative skip other than 423 that didn't put me to sleep. Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big fan of this take on the Pataphysics Department - both the idea that they do not actually exist and are fictional themselves, but also the idea that they also deal with anomalous fiction, not just fourth wall breakage. There's a lot of room to that concept.

It's also good that at no point does this break the fourth wall. This exists completely on its own.

I uh… you do realize this was the premise of the entire original OverMeta written by Rimple on his author page? That Pataphysics doesn't actually exist and is a fictional entity in and of itself using fictional characters to effect change through different layers of fictional reality, while also dealing with fictional anomalies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely, it isn't up it's own arse with confusion and doesn't reference the reader.

Hippo is god now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pataphysics Department (a fictitious department created for the purposes of investigating, counteracting, and containing allegorical and/or metafictional anomalies)

Big fan of this take on the Pataphysics Department - both the idea that they do not actually exist and are fictional themselves, but also the idea that they also deal with anomalous fiction, not just fourth wall breakage. There's a lot of room to that concept.

It's also good that at no point does this break the fourth wall. This exists completely on its own.

The cause or motivation behind this phenomenon remains unknown.

Oh come on. Even if we didn't just go through two iterations of Don Quixote, the Foundation should be able to draw the "Mancha+Windmills" connection pretty damn easy. Perhaps changing unknown to uncertain, which would allow for the idea that the Foundation has some inkling of what's going on, while keeping the ending intact?

I also think the last iteration could've worked as a nice short and sweet <500 word skip, but it's better as the ending here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hippo is a proud man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why the proud Foundation logo?

Edit: Well.. Is there an in-universe explaination for this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw "pataphysics" and groaned; kept reading when I saw Don Quixote. By the end realized I actually kinda love this in spite of myself; upvoted. Went to the comments and saw it was by the Great Hippo and everything made sense. Great work as always.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a goddamn masterpiece, and one of your absolute best skips. Take all the +1s.

Of course the doctor would be called "Pierre Menard" :D :D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I honestly spent a lot of time debating whether or not Don Quixote ought to meet the Triple Moon Initiative. I couldn't figure out if they'd fight him, give him a fist-bump as they passed by, or arm him with every form of weaponry known to man.

Also, did Don Quixote just defeat the Daevite Empire? Is 140 neutralized in this continuity now?

The implication here is that this is all just happening in the IntSCPFN servers; none of the entries are actually occurring. The Foundation is just freaking out because some madman is running through their servers and rewriting their files at his leisure.

That being said, I won't claim the idea of Don Quixote vs the Daevite empire didn't cross my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initially I thought this was a bit derivative of the Triple Moon Initiative SCP where they alter movies, but I love how it turned into a story about Don Q fighting his way through SCP files, the reveal that Don Q is actually Sancho, Sancho nearly dying until the real Don Q saves him, and then the hero returns home (as he always should), happily spending his days fighting windmills (because they might be giants).

I'm seriously a sucker for a happy ending, and I love that final iteration. Whole-hearted upvote.

Also, did Don Quixote just defeat the Daevite Empire? Is 140 neutralized in this continuity now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, to be fair, all they really had to hide were the copies of books he was altering. It's not like he's altering all of fiction — just one single book at a time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take notes people. This is how you do pataphysics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The remainder of the poem is a debate between the narrator and Alonso regarding who is lovelier: the narrator's lost Lenore, or Alonso's beloved Dulcinea. It concludes with a fist-fight.

Upvoted on the spot

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s a bit long, and a bit contrived that they could hide the fact that one of the most influential characters in history is anomalous, but I enjoyed the read.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…there, uh, is no pun at the end?

(Either way, I kind of figured you wouldn't like it — so no worries.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding BaronJoe

I'm also not a fan of pataphysics and I really hated the pun at the end

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers.
– hide block

Dr. Pierre Menard (the head of the Pataphysics Department, and the author of all the footnotes) is, in actuality, Don Quixote — or Alonso Quixano. The 'Alonso Quixano' the article keeps referring to is, in actuality, Sancho Panza — Don Quixote's long-suffering squire (now a valiant knight himself). The article is effectively a protracted rescue mission carried out by Sancho to re-ignite the sense of adventure of his old master, who has given up his dreams and become a depressing, cynical busy-body who works for the Foundation's Pataphysics Department.

In the first iteration, Alonso (again, this is Sancho, remember) infects fiction after he discovers the Foundation from Fred (and realizes that clearly, his old Master has been 'kidnapped' by them). He's basically accelerating through fiction, trying to reach the Foundation.

In the second iteration, he's managed to do it — he's infected the Foundation's database, and proceeds forward through each article (in order) until he reaches the one we're currently reading (which is also the one Pierre Menard — who is the actual Don Quixote — is responsible for editing and writing). Bloodied and nearly dead, he offers his sword to his old Master, who embraces his near-dead squire.

The final iteration is meant to imply that Pierre has returned to his old ways, alongside his squire — now as windmill keepers, where they regularly attack the windmills (on account of suspecting they might be giants).
